content:
{
  "title": "Graph-of-Thought Prompting: Structural Stepwise Reasoning for LLM Mathematical Problem Solving",
  "problem": "Large language models struggle with complex mathematical problems that require structured, multi-path reasoning, often failing when reasoning steps involve dependencies, backtracking, or case analysis. Existing prompting techniques elicit linear or sketched chains of reasoning, but do not sufficiently model the non-linear structure of genuine mathematical thinking.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLMs' ability to solve advanced mathematical problems has high impact for education, research, and automation of scientific discovery. As mathematical reasoning often involves branching, merging, and revisiting prior assumptions, there is an urgent need for prompting methods that explicitly encourage such structured exploration. Existing methods either focus on linear stepwise reasoning or dataset construction, missing the non-sequential nature of human mathematical cognition.",
  "method": "We propose Graph-of-Thought (GoT) prompting, in which the LLM is guided to represent its evolving solution as an explicit graph structure: each node contains a mathematical subgoal, claim, or partial result, and edges denote logical dependencies or transitions (including case splits or backtracking). Prompts instruct the model to iteratively expand, update, or revise nodes and edges as it explores the problem. The method leverages custom formatting and recursive prompt templates to maintain and update the reasoning graph. At the end, the LLM is prompted to synthesize a final answer using the constructed graph as an explicit scaffold.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate GoT prompting on challenging benchmarks such as MATH, GSM8K, and CHAMP, focusing on problems involving proofs, casework, or non-linear solution paths. Baselines will include Chain-of-Thought, Sketch-of-Thought, and self-correcting prompts. Metrics will assess solution accuracy, completeness of reasoning, and structural fidelity to ground-truth mathematical reasoning graphs."
}
